bigQuestion: Where are we in the universe?

words.definition_en: Bright object that orbits around Earth.
words.definition_zh: 繞地球運轉的明亮天體

words.definition_en: Small rocky object floating in space.
words.definition_zh: 漂浮在太空中的小岩石

words.definition_en: Icy space object with a long bright tail.
words.definition_zh: 有長尾巴的冰凍太空物體

words.definition_en: Rock from space that falls to Earth.
words.definition_zh: 從太空掉落到地球的岩石

words.definition_en: The sun and all planets around it.
words.definition_zh: 太陽和圍繞它的所有行星

words.definition_en: Huge balls of hot glowing gas in space.
words.definition_zh: 太空中閃閃發光的巨大熾熱氣體球

words.definition_en: Huge group of stars and space dust.
words.definition_zh: 由許多星星和塵埃組成的龐大星系

words.definition_en: Everything in space including all galaxies.
words.definition_zh: 包括所有星系的整個太空

words.definition_en: Vehicle that travels through outer space.
words.definition_zh: 在外太空中旅行的交通工具

words.definition_en: Tool to see far away stars clearly.
words.definition_zh: 用來清楚看遠方星星的工具

words.definition_en: Building with telescope for studying space.
words.definition_zh: 用望遠鏡研究太空的建築物

examples: I will visit those places again, Bella thought.
Scientists will find new solar systems in our galaxy.
Scientists will send a person to Mars someday.
She will become an astronaut when she grows up.
rules: Use will to make a prediction.
A prediction is something you believe about the future.
A prediction is not a fact.

bigQuestion: Where are we in the universe?

words.definition_en: Scientist who studies stars and planets.
Robot spacecraft that explores outer space.
The very center part of something.
Force that pulls things down to Earth.
Path that planets take around the sun.
Anything that takes up space and has weight.
How far apart two things are from each other.
Straight line across the middle of a circle.
The outside or top layer of something.
Large round holes made by impacts or explosions.
One of a kind and different from everything else.

examples: If technology continues to grow, we will travel to these places ourselves.
We will travel to these places ourselves if technology continues to grow.
If I become an astronaut, I will walk on the surface of Mars.
If I study hard, I will get good grades.
rules: Use the future real conditional to talk about a future possibility and what will happen as a result of it.
Use the word if with a future possibility.
The if clause can come first or second in the sentence.

bigQuestion: How do we know what happened long ago?

words.definition_en: Large group of soldiers fighting together.
People trained to fight and protect.
Special clothes soldiers wear alike.
Powerful ruler of a country.
Metal protection clothing worn in battle.
Valuable things like gold and jewels.
Scientist who studies old things underground.
Underground place where dead people buried.
Hard green stone used for jewelry.
Wet dirt used to make pottery.
Poor person working on farms long ago.

examples: The Chinese government plans to keep it closed for now.
All the students promised to find more terracotta soldiers.
The archaeologists tried to build the clay army.
I forgot to pack my lunch.
rules: Some verbs are often followed by an infinitive.
An infinitive is a verb with the word to in front of it.
Common verbs followed by infinitives include: plan, promise, try, forget, want, decide, need.

bigQuestion: How do we know what happened long ago?

words.definition_en: Huge extinct reptiles that lived long ago.
The hard bone that protects your brain.
A deep narrow valley with steep sides.
To look at something very carefully.
To find something that was hidden or unknown.
To dig carefully in the ground for artifacts.
Sheets of material stacked on top of each other.
Scientist who studies ancient fossils and dinosaurs.
Gray powder left after something burns completely.
Rock formed from compressed layers of sediment.
Activity you do for fun in your spare time.

examples: Javier enjoyed playing with his friends.
When it began raining, the excavation team went inside.
The museum staff finished cleaning the dinosaur bones.
I enjoy swimming in a pool on a hot day.
rules: Gerunds are verbs that end in -ing.
Gerunds can act like nouns.
Verbs like enjoy, finish, and practice are often followed by gerunds.

bigQuestion: Where does our food come from?

words.definition_en: Tall plant that makes sweet sugar.
Grain crop used to make bread.
Brown spice that smells warm and sweet.
Soft yellow food made from milk cream.
Sweet flavor used in cakes and ice cream.
Things you mix together to make food.
Hard outer covering of a tree trunk.
Large farm where one crop is grown.
Big boat powered by steam engine.
To go bad and not be good anymore.
Wild spotted cat from Africa and Asia.

examples: I'm making a special breakfast today.
We're playing soccer after school today.
My parents are having dinner at a French restaurant tomorrow.
I'm cleaning my bedroom on Saturday morning.
rules: The present continuous tense can tell about future actions that someone plans.
Include the future time when you use this tense.
Use am/is/are + verb-ing to form the present continuous.

topic: Food and Farming: The World Around Us
bigQuestion: Where does our food come from?

words.definition_en: Easy and quick to do or use.
Send goods to another country to sell.
From or near your own town.
Steps to make or do something.
A box or wrapper around something.
Place where farmers sell fresh food.
Growing crops and raising animals for food.
Large farm owned by big companies.
Become smaller or less in amount.
One hundred years of time.
Substance used to clean, treat, or mix.

examples: Would you like to know where your food comes from?
Would you like a delicious apple from South Africa?
Would you like to bake a cake with me?
Would you like a piece of apple pie?
rules: Use Would you like ... ? to make a polite offer.
You can use this phrase to offer an action or a thing.
Would you like + to + verb (action) or Would you like + noun (thing).

words.definition_en: A quick, simple drawing done with pencil.
Soft colored sticks used for drawing and coloring.
words.definition_zh: 用來畫畫和著色的軟色棒

words.definition_en: Thick cloth used for painting pictures on.
Tools with bristles for putting paint on surfaces.
words.definition_zh: 用來塗油漆的刷子工具

words.definition_en: The outline or form of an object or thing.
words.definition_zh: 物體或東西的輪廓或形式

words.definition_en: Thin cord made by twisting fibers together.
words.definition_zh: 由纖維擰在一起製成的細繩

words.definition_en: Having length, width, and height or depth.
A young person with amazing talent or ability.
words.definition_zh: 有驚人才能或能力的年輕人

words.definition_en: An artist who creates paintings on sidewalks outside.
words.definition_zh: 在街道上創作繪畫的藝術家

words.definition_en: A person who builds and repairs things from wood.
words.definition_zh: 用木頭製造和修理東西的人

words.definition_en: An artist who carves shapes from stone or clay.
words.definition_zh: 從石頭或黏土中雕刻形狀的藝術家

examples: Can anyone make art?
I want to paint something in this room.
Everyone worked on the sculpture.
There is something I like about this sculpture.
rules: Use indefinite pronouns to talk about unnamed people or things.
Anyone means any of the people.
Something means one of the things.
Everyone means all of the people.

words.definition_en: A show of artwork for people to see.
A border that holds and displays a picture.
A picture or view of nature and outdoors.
How something feels when you touch it.
Using darker colors to show shadows in art.
How things look from where you stand.
Big differences between light and dark colors.
The empty area around objects in artwork.
Marked or colored by something spilled on it.
Very bright and shining with lots of light.
Very light in color, not bright or dark.

examples: "Shall I show you some more sketches?" he asks.
"I will sign you up for something," his mother offers.
Shall I bring you a cold drink?
I'll carry that for you.
rules: Use shall to make an offer with a question.
Use will to make an offer with a statement.
Shall I ...? is used to offer to do something for someone.

words.definition_en: Water pathway built for boats to travel.
Place where ships load and unload cargo.
The style and design of buildings.
Small vehicle pulled by a person or bike.
Person selling things on the street.
Very old valuable objects from long ago.
Small gifts to remember a special place.
Colorful cloth made with wax and dye.
Unusual fruits from faraway tropical places.
Large fish often used for food.
Small sea creature with a curved tail.

examples: Do you have nothing to do?
No one could see everything.
Nobody is in the museum.
There is nothing in my pocket.
rules: Indefinite pronouns refer to people or objects that we don't name or know.
Negative indefinite pronouns begin with no-.
Nothing means not anything; no one and nobody mean no person.

words.definition_en: People who live in a city or country.
People who help others without getting paid.
The leader in charge of a city.
Someone who helps another person do their job.
Group of people who make rules for a city.
The main building where city government works.
The leader chosen to run a country.
People who pick up trash from streets.
Tools and machines needed to do something.
When leaders tell reporters important information.
A safe outdoor place where kids can play.

examples: You'll work hard, won't you?
Marcy wasn't nervous, was she?
Ann is the youngest student, isn't she?
You're ready for the test, aren't you?
rules: In a tag question, a statement is turned into a question by adding a tag at the end.
A tag question always has a yes or no answer.
Tag questions are often used to say things that we think are true, but that we don't know for sure.

words.definition_en: Tiny living parts that make up bodies.
Tool that makes tiny things look big.
Super tiny germ that makes you sick.
Tiny living things that can cause illness.
Sickness that makes your body not work right.
Serious flu illness with fever and body aches.
Mild illness with stuffy nose and cough.
Sticky wet stuff from nose when sick.
Your body's ability to fight off germs.
When body parts cannot move or work.
When germs enter and make you sick.

examples: Max Axiom studies these fascinating, tiny visitors.
The doctor washed his white, new lab coat.
Max saw round, red, large viruses.
There is a long, white, plastic ruler on my desk.
rules: You can use more than one adjective to describe a noun.
The adjectives must go in a certain order: opinion ... size ... age ... shape ... color ... kind.
Opinion adjectives come before size adjectives.

words.definition_en: Group of parts working together.
Red liquid that flows in body.
words.definition_zh: 在身體裡流動的紅色液體

words.definition_en: Muscle that pumps blood around body.
words.definition_zh: 將血液泵送全身的肌肉

words.definition_en: Tubes that carry blood from heart.
words.definition_zh: 從心臟運送血液的管子

words.definition_en: Tubes that bring blood back to heart.
words.definition_zh: 將血液送回心臟的管子

words.definition_en: Tiny blood vessels connecting arteries veins.
words.definition_zh: 連接動脈和靜脈的微小血管

words.definition_en: Organs that help us breathe air.
Tube that carries air to lungs.
words.definition_zh: 將空氣運送到肺的管子

words.definition_en: Muscle below lungs that helps breathing.
words.definition_zh: 肺下方幫助呼吸的肌肉

words.definition_en: Waste gas our body breathes out.
Heartbeat you can feel in wrist.
words.definition_zh: 你在手腕感受到的心跳

examples: In ancient times, people used to think differently about the human body.
When I was young, I used to ride my bike.
My sister used to go to the swimming pool often as a child.
I used to take the bus to school.
rules: Use used to before an action that happened often in the past, but not now.
Used to describes a past habit or state that no longer exists.
Compare with simple past: simple past describes one completed action; used to describes repeated past actions.

words.definition_en: Ways to share information with many people.
Paper with news and stories printed daily.
Colorful booklet with stories and pictures.
Machine that copies words onto paper.
Company that makes and sells books.
Showing products to get people to buy.
Send show on radio or television.
Connected network of information online.
Small computer you carry in pocket.
Online diary where people share thoughts.
Website where people connect and talk.

grammar.point: Must, Mustn't, Have To, Don't Have To
examples: You must pay for the Internet to use it at home.
You mustn't take a newspaper without paying for it.
You don't have to carry a computer to check the news.
You must walk quietly in the halls.
rules: Use must and have to to talk about what a person needs to do.
Use mustn't to talk about what a person shouldn't do.
Use don't have to to talk about what a person doesn't need to do.

words.definition_en: Person who finds and tells news stories.
Person who checks and fixes writing work.
When you ask someone questions to learn.
Big title words at top of story.
Brave person who helps others and does good.
Place on internet where you find information.
Something you did wrong by accident.
Choose what you want to do next.
Talk together about ideas and thoughts.
Look carefully to find out the truth.
Look hard for something you cannot find.

examples: I have decided to keep a blog.
He hasn't written a blog before.
The reporter has done many interviews.
I haven't met a reporter before.
rules: Use the present perfect tense to tell what happened in the past, but did not happen at a specific time.
Use the past participle form of the main verb with has or have.
Use hasn't / haven't for negative sentences.

bigQuestion: What can we learn from nature's power?

words.definition_en: When water covers land after heavy rain.
When the ground shakes and moves violently.
Huge ocean waves caused by earthquakes underwater.
Very strong spinning storm with powerful winds.
Spinning funnel cloud that destroys things below.
Heavy snowstorm with very strong cold winds.
Storm with lightning, thunder, and heavy rain.
Person who helps save people from danger.
Person hurt or harmed by something bad.
Wires that bring electricity to homes and buildings.
When a building falls down suddenly.

grammar.point: Present Perfect with Ever and Never
examples: Have you ever seen a real tornado?
Yes, I have. / No, I haven't. I've never seen a real tornado.
Have you ever experienced an earthquake?
I've never felt an earthquake.
rules: Use the present perfect with ever and never to ask and answer questions about life experiences.
Use ever in questions to ask about any time up to now.
Use never in statements to say something has not happened at any time.

bigQuestion: What can we learn from nature's power?

words.definition_en: A sudden dangerous situation needing quick help.
Safe place to go during bad storms.
Box with supplies to help injured people.
Paper money and coins you can spend.
Strong covers that protect windows from storms.
Warm bag you zip up to sleep inside.
Handheld light that works with batteries.
Small power sources that make things work.
Things you need and keep for emergencies.
Clean drinking water in sealed plastic bottles.
Food in metal containers that lasts long.

grammar.point: Present Perfect with Already, Just, and Yet
examples: I've already packed everything.
I've just eaten dinner.
Have you cleaned your room yet? No, I haven't cleaned it yet.
I've already made twelve sandwiches and four pizzas.
rules: You can use the present perfect with the adverbs already, just, and yet.
These adverbs tell when something happened or didn't happen.
Already and just are used in positive statements; yet is used in questions and negative statements.

words.definition_en: A large area with similar plants and animals.
A very dry place with little rain.
A large area covered mostly with grass.
A warm wet forest with many plants.
A forest with four seasons and mild weather.
A cold forest with many evergreen trees.
A very cold place with frozen ground.
Water that has no salt in it.
An imaginary line around the middle of Earth.
A slow-moving animal that lives in trees.
A plant with spines that lives in deserts.

grammar.point: Present Perfect with For and Since
examples: These biomes have provided a home for animals for a long time.
I have studied biomes since February.
My dad has studied mammals for twenty years.
The government has protected this forest since 1998.
rules: Use the present perfect with for and since to show that something began in the past and is still happening now.
Use for to talk about an amount of time.
Use since to talk about a specific point of time in the past.

words.definition_en: Colorful fish that eats coral.
Tiny horse-shaped sea animal.
Huge flat fish with wide wings.
Ancient reptile with shell living in ocean.
Place to see ocean animals and plants.
Tube to breathe while swimming underwater.
Very fast boat that goes on water.
Line between two different countries.
Written request signed by many people.
Official rule everyone must follow.
To surprise someone very much.

examples: Camila has created a petition.
When she got home, Camila created a petition.
I have run in a race. I ran in a race last summer.
They have snorkeled this morning.
rules: Use the present perfect for past experiences, past actions that continue to the present, or actions that happened at an unspecific time.
Use the simple past for completed actions that happened at a specific time.
The simple past is used when we know when something happened.
